Name: jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping for Efficient Latency-Aware Inference
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging the Jacobian matrix to dynamically skip layers during inference, we can achieve significant latency reduction without compromising model accuracy, particularly in latency-critical applications.
Related Work: Existing work on layer skipping primarily focuses on static pruning or heuristic-based approaches. Techniques like adaptive inference and early exiting have been explored, but they often lack a rigorous theoretical foundation or fail to account for the dynamic nature of inference latency. Our proposal distinguishes itself by using the Jacobian matrix to inform skipping decisions, ensuring that skipped layers minimally perturb the model's output.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to reduce inference latency by dynamically skipping layers based on the Jacobian matrix. This method leverages the local sensitivity of the model's output to each layer's activation, allowing us to identify and skip layers that contribute minimally to the final prediction. We introduce a PAC-Bayes framework to bound the error introduced by layer skipping, ensuring robustness. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves significant latency reduction while maintaining high accuracy, making it suitable for latency-critical applications.
Experiments: Jacobian Calculation: Compute the Jacobian matrix for each layer during inference to assess its impact on the output.
Layer Skipping Criteria: Develop a threshold-based criterion to decide which layers to skip based on the Jacobian matrix.
PAC-Bayes Bound: Derive a PAC-Bayes bound to quantify the error introduced by layer skipping.
Benchmark Evaluation: Evaluate the method on standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) using metrics such as inference latency, accuracy, and memory usage.
Ablation Studies: Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different thresholds and layer skipping strategies.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of calculating the Jacobian matrix during inference might offset the latency gains.
The method may not generalize well to all architectures or tasks.
The PAC-Bayes bound might be too conservative, limiting the practical applicability of the method.
